lesson.title: VibeCode: Browser-to-Backend Communication
lesson.description: Learn how data flows from a Chrome extension to a FastAPI backend
learningObjectives: Understand why CORS middleware is necessary for browser-backend communication
Learn Pydantic data models and validation

content.headline: Your browser has a bodyguard.
content.explanation: By default, web browsers enforce a **Same-Origin Policy**. This means a webpage from `claude.ai` cannot make requests to `localhost:8000` without permission. It's a security feature to prevent malicious scripts from stealing your data.
content.scenario: Imagine this: You're on Claude.ai asking for code. The page tries to send that code to your local Python backend. Your browser blocks it—not because of a bug, but because a malicious website could do the same thing to steal your banking info.
content.solution: Enter **CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)**. This is a protocol that says: 'It's okay, I give permission.' When your FastAPI backend includes CORS middleware, it tells the browser: 'I'm safe, you can talk to me.'

codeHighlight.snippet:
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["*"],  # Allow all origins (for development)
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)

content.keyTakeaway: Without this middleware, your Chrome extension's `fetch()` call would fail silently. The browser would say: 'No, I don't know if that backend is trustworthy.'

quiz.question: Why can't your Chrome extension talk to `localhost:8000` without CORS middleware?

options.explanation: Firewalls operate at the network layer. CORS is a browser-level security feature.

options.text: The browser's Same-Origin Policy blocks cross-origin requests.
options.explanation: Correct! The browser sees `claude.ai` (origin) trying to talk to `localhost:8000` (different origin) and blocks it for security.
FastAPI absolutely supports HTTP. The issue is the browser policy, not the backend.

options.text: You need to use WebSockets instead.
options.explanation: WebSockets still require CORS headers. This is a browser security requirement, not a protocol issue.

steps.title: 🧩 The Logic: Pydantic Models & Data Validation

content.headline: Every message needs a shape.
content.explanation: When your extension sends data to the backend, both sides need to agree: 'What data are we sending? What are the types?' That's where **Pydantic models** come in. They're like blueprints that say: 'I expect a string for code, a string for context,' etc.

modelBreakdown.name: CodeIngestion
modelBreakdown.description: This model defines the structure of code that flows from your browser to the backend.

fields.name: code
fields.description: The raw source code extracted from the AI chat

fields.name: context
fields.description: The prompt or conversation context that preceded the code
fields.example: Write a simple Python hello world function

fields.name: url
fields.description: The URL of the AI chat page (e.g., claude.ai, chatgpt.com)

fields.name: source_model
fields.description: The AI model that generated the code (Claude, GPT-4, etc.)

codeHighlight.snippet:
class CodeIngestion(BaseModel):
    code: str
    context: str
    url: str
    source_model: Optional[str] = "Unknown"

exercise.title: Build the CodeIngestion Model
exercise.description: Fill in the missing field definitions in the Pydantic model. The code on the left shows what's sent from the browser.

given.code:
class CodeIngestion(BaseModel):
    code: ___________
    ___________: str
    url: ___________
    source_model: Optional[___________] = "Unknown"

blanks.hint: What data type is code? It's text, so it's a Python...
This field holds the conversation text preceding the code.
URLs are text strings. What type?
The model name (Claude, GPT, etc.) is text, so it's a...

whyMatters: Pydantic **validates** incoming data. If the browser sends malformed data, FastAPI rejects it automatically.
**Type hints** make your code self-documenting. Any developer can look at this model and understand what data flows through.
**Optional fields** let you make some data required and some optional. Here, `source_model` is optional—the backend will use 'Unknown' as a default if not provided.

steps.title: 🌊 The Flow: Data Journey Visualization

content.headline: Follow the data from browser to backend.

dataJourney.action: Extract code block and context from the DOM
Prepare data and send HTTP POST to backend
Receive request and validate with Pydantic

data.message: Project data cached. Ready for deconstruction.

dataJourney.code:
const result = await response.json();
updateStatus(`✨ Success! Project ID: ${result.project_id}`, 'success');

segments.description: Browser searches DOM for code blocks
HTTP POST from browser to backend

keyPoints: The browser initiates the journey by asking content.js to grab data
Pydantic validates the data shape before FastAPI even runs your handler function
CORS headers tell the browser the response is allowed to reach the extension
The entire round-trip happens in ~215ms—fast enough for a smooth user experience

summary.title: You've Completed Phase 0! 🎉
recap: ✅ Understood why CORS middleware bridges the browser-backend gap
✅ Learned Pydantic models enforce data structure and type safety
✅ Visualized the complete data journey from extension to backend
summary.nextPhaseDescription: Now that data reaches your backend, we'll build the 'teaching engine'—using Claude's 3.1 Pro model to analyze the ingested code and generate interactive lessons automatically.

actionItems.title: Test the Pipeline
actionItems.description: Run the backend, load the extension, and successfully send code from an AI chat to your backend. Watch the terminal logs.

actionItems.title: Modify CodeIngestion Model
actionItems.description: Add a new optional field to CodeIngestion (e.g., `language: Optional[str]`) and update the extension to capture it.

actionItems.title: Explore CORS in Depth
actionItems.description: Research what happens when you remove the CORS middleware. Try making a fetch call—what error do you see?
